notes: Gene-specific rescue-literature dossier for the 20 Quiver-moat TSC2-KO rescue-gene candidates. Mechanistic tiering: Tier 1 (MCRS1, RALA, INO80) are mechanistically proximal to the TSC2-RHEB-mTORC1 axis; Tier 2 (BCL2, VMP1, SMARCB1, TBL1XR1) are functionally adjacent (autophagy/CNS); Tier 3 plausible-but-distant; Tier 4 (BANF1, CSTF3, DIDO1, GPN3) no meaningful mTOR/autophagy/TSC link (likely signature noise). The moat's top signature-reversers (DCTN6, FZD7, CSTF3, GPN3) are mostly Tier 3-4 — the literature re-prioritises lower-signature genes (MCRS1 #11, RALA #12, INO80 #19) as the strongest mechanistic rescuers.

evidence.claim: MCRS1 (TIER 1, top mechanistic candidate): directly tethers RHEB to the lysosomal surface in an amino-acid-dependent manner, coupling RHEB to mTORC1 activation; MCRS1 depletion promotes GDP-bound RHEB and delocalises RHEB from the lysosome to endocytic recycling vesicles, inactivating mTORC1 — even when RHEB is GTP-loaded (as in TSC2-KO where TSC2 GAP activity is absent). This is the precise step TSC2 normally regulates. MCRS1-null zebrafish show reduced brain/eye size and retinal lamination failure, paralleling TSC cortical/neuronal disorganisation.
evidence.source: Fawal 2015 Dev Cell; Garrido 2016 Small GTPases; Lee 2016 Sci Rep

evidence.claim: RALA (TIER 1): a direct positive mTORC1 regulator (a small GTPase that activates mTORC1, distinct from the Rag/RHEB axis), so it provides an additive mTORC1 input that could be dampened to counteract TSC2-KO hyperactivation. A somatic gain-of-function RALA variant (c.G482A) found in brain tissue causes focal cortical dysplasia type IIb — pathologically identical to TSC cortical tubers — via mTOR hyperactivation (elevated p-S6) and abnormal cortical neuronal migration. Ral GTPase ablation impairs mTORC1-dependent myelination.
evidence.source: Dunlop & Tee 2009 Cell Signal; Zhu & Wang 2020 J Nutr; Xu 2022 Front Behav Neurosci; DeGeer 2022 Cell Rep

evidence.claim: INO80 (TIER 1): sits in the direct transcriptional output arm of TORC1 signalling. TORC1 activates the Rpd3L deacetylase, which deacetylates INO80 (K929) and protects it from autophagic degradation; stabilised INO80 evicts H2A.Z from autophagy (ATG) gene promoters, repressing them. TORC1 inhibition or INO80 suppression de-represses ATG genes — so suppressing INO80 would induce autophagy transcriptionally EVEN under hyperactive mTORC1, bypassing the RHEB/mTORC1 kinase step. INO80-class remodellers are recurrently mutated in neurodevelopmental disorders.
evidence.source: Li 2023 Autophagy; Li 2023 Sci Adv; Malone & Roberts 2024 Nat Rev Drug Discov

evidence.claim: BCL2 (TIER 2): the canonical autophagy gatekeeper — it binds the BH3 domain of Beclin-1 to block PI3KC3 assembly and autophagosome nucleation; mTORC1 status modulates BCL2 phosphorylation. Downregulating BCL2 de-represses Beclin-1 and robustly induces autophagy regardless of upstream mTORC1 hyperactivation, a credible pharmacological rescue node operating downstream/parallel to RHEB.
evidence.source: Pattingre 2005 / Xu & Qin 2019 Adv Exp Med Biol; Zhu 2024 J Neuroinflammation

evidence.claim: VMP1 (TIER 2): an ER-resident phospholipid scramblase essential for autophagosome nucleation (PI3P/Beclin-1 platform); its loss impairs autophagosome formation and triggers NLRP3 neuroinflammation, with altered expression in Parkinson's disease and MS. Restoring/upregulating VMP1 would stimulate autophagosome nucleation, bypassing mTORC1-mediated autophagy suppression in TSC2-KO.
evidence.source: Calvo-Garrido 2014 Traffic; Zhang 2021 Autophagy; Wang 2020 Front Mol Neurosci

evidence.claim: SMARCB1 (TIER 2): a SWI/SNF subunit whose loss activates mTOR in schwannomatosis/rhabdoid tumours (CNS tumour driver, incl. AT/RT); SMARCB1-deficient cells show autophagy/proteostasis vulnerabilities. A connectivity-map rescue would require SMARCB1 upregulation suppressing mTOR output — plausible via chromatin regulation but not directly demonstrated.
evidence.source: Noujaim 2015 Front Oncol; Tamura 2021 Int J Mol Sci; Msaouel 2019 Oncotarget

evidence.claim: TBL1XR1 (TIER 2): an NCoR/SMRT corepressor subunit and a strongly validated autism/epilepsy/intellectual-disability gene (de novo mutations recurrent in ASD; haploinsufficiency causes ID); regulates Wnt/beta-catenin (which mTORC1 activates) and the MAPK cascade in neural progenitors. CNS phenotypes closely overlap TSC comorbidities; pathway-level mTOR intersection via Wnt.
evidence.source: O'Roak 2012 Science; Pons 2015 Am J Med Genet; Mastrototaro 2021 Front Cell Dev Biol

evidence.claim: BAP1 (TIER 3): H2A deubiquitinase (PR-DUB complex); germline missense variants cause a syndromic neurodevelopmental disorder; mTOR pathway altered in BAP1-mutant mesothelioma. Plausible chromatin-level link to autophagy gene accessibility, but no direct mTORC1 mechanism.
evidence.source: Kury 2022 Am J Hum Genet; Bueno 2016 Nat Genet

evidence.claim: RRAD (TIER 3): a Ras-superfamily GTPase that in glioblastoma promotes EGFR->STAT3/AKT signalling (RRAD->AKT->mTOR is plausible since AKT phosphorylates TSC2), and suppresses NF-kB/Warburg metabolism. Indirect mTOR link in brain-tumour cells; no direct TSC mechanistic evidence.
evidence.source: Yeom 2014 Mol Cancer Ther; Liu 2015 Oncotarget

evidence.claim: FZD7 (TIER 3): a Wnt receptor; mTOR and Wnt/beta-catenin cross-talk extensively (mTORC1 stabilises beta-catenin), and FZD7 is itself a beta-catenin target. Pathway-level mTOR crosstalk only; no direct TSC/RHEB-axis evidence.
evidence.source: Inoki 2006 / Prossomariti 2020; Ben-Moshe 2019 Nat Metab

evidence.claim: TAF12 (TIER 3): in fission yeast, Taf12 (in the SAGA coactivator) is a direct phosphorylation target of both TORC1 and TORC2, integrating nutrient signalling into transcription — a genuine mechanistic TORC1 link, but only established in yeast; mammalian mTORC1 regulation of TAF12 is unpublished.
evidence.source: Laboucarie 2017 EMBO Rep

evidence.claim: DPM2 (TIER 3): regulatory subunit of dolichol-phosphate-mannose synthase (ER N-glycosylation / GPI anchors); DPM2-CDG causes severe CNS disease (intractable epilepsy, progressive microcephaly, dystroglycanopathy). Strong neurological relevance overlapping TSC seizures, but no mechanistic mTORC1/RHEB link (the dolichol pathway does not affect RHEB farnesylation).
evidence.source: Barone 2012 Ann Neurol; recent DPM2-CDG series

evidence.claim: RPL4 (TIER 3): an essential large-subunit ribosomal protein; sits DOWNSTREAM of mTORC1 (the master regulator of ribosome biogenesis, a hallmark of the TSC proliferative phenotype). RPL4 depletion triggers ribosomal stress -> RPL5/RPL11-dependent p53 activation (indirect autophagy induction). No causal upstream role in mTOR regulation.
evidence.source: He 2016 Oncotarget

evidence.claim: VPS54 (TIER 3): the unique GARP-complex subunit governing endosome->TGN retrograde transport and lysosomal-enzyme sorting; its hypomorphic mutation defines the wobbler mouse (motor-neuron-disease model). Strong lysosomal-trafficking and CNS relevance; lysosome->mTORC1 connection plausible but not demonstrated for VPS54.
evidence.source: Schmitt-John 2015 Front Neurosci; Perez-Victoria 2010

evidence.claim: VPS52 (TIER 3): shared GARP/EARP subunit (vesicle tethering to TGN/recycling endosomes); depletion impairs retrograde trafficking, lysosomal enzyme sorting and autophagy, and interacts with LRRK2 (Parkinson's). Indirect mTOR relevance via lysosomal function; required for dendrite morphogenesis.
evidence.source: Perez-Victoria 2010; Beilina 2020 Cell Rep; O'Brien 2023 J Cell Biol

evidence.claim: DCTN6 (TIER 3, moat rank #1 by signature): dynactin subunit; dynein/dynactin retrograde transport governs lysosomal positioning (which influences mTORC1 at the lysosome) and autophagosome-lysosome fusion, but not shown specifically for DCTN6. DCTN6 declines with brain ageing and its deficiency enhances dystrophic-neurite formation — cytoskeletal disorganisation echoing TSC dysmorphic neurons. No direct mTOR/TSC link.
evidence.source: Liu 2016 Front Cell Dev Biol; Sharoar 2021 Neurobiol Aging

evidence.claim: BANF1 (TIER 4): Barrier-to-Autointegration Factor; nuclear-envelope reassembly and rupture repair; mutations cause Nestor-Guillermo progeria (laminopathy). No published mTOR, autophagy, or TSC connection — likely connectivity-map noise.
evidence.source: Jamin & Wiebe 2015 Curr Opin Cell Biol

evidence.claim: CSTF3 (TIER 4, moat rank #4 by signature): cleavage-stimulation / alternative-polyadenylation factor; a single ovarian-cancer study correlates CSTF3/NEAT1_1 downregulation with PI3K/AKT/mTOR inactivation, but this is correlative with no mechanistic or neuronal evidence — likely signature noise.
evidence.source: Luo 2024 Cell Death Dis

evidence.claim: DIDO1 (TIER 4): Death Inducer-Obliterator; apoptosis regulator; rare LOF variants associate with increased BMI in women (GWAS). No mTOR/autophagy/TSC mechanistic connection — likely signature noise.
evidence.source: Kaisinger 2023 Cell Genomics

evidence.claim: GPN3 (TIER 4, moat rank #6 by signature): GPN-loop GTPase; one paper shows it inhibits clathrin-mediated EGFR endocytosis in NSCLC (EGFR can feed PI3K/AKT/mTOR, but untested in TSC). No neurological or TSC/mTOR relevance — likely signature noise.
evidence.source: Xu 2025 Cell Death Discov
